service: Educate and empower New Yorkers with low and moderate incomes.
oneLiner: This work matters because hundreds of thousands of NYC households have no bank account and are pushed toward costly check-cashers and predatory lenders, which is why the City created the nation's first municipal Office of Financial Empowerment in 2006 to offer free financial counseling instead.
summary: The Office of Financial Empowerment, launched in 2006 within the Department of Consumer Affairs (now DCWP), was the first local-government initiative in the country dedicated to educating, empowering and protecting people with low incomes. Its Financial Empowerment Centers, which opened starting in 2008, provide free one-on-one financial counseling and have served tens of thousands of New Yorkers, helping clients reduce debt and build savings. The program responds to a persistent problem: DCWP's own research has repeatedly found hundreds of thousands of NYC households are unbanked or underbanked, concentrated in the city's lowest-income communities.

sources.label: Q&A with Cathie Mahon, head of the Office of Financial Empowerment - Center for an Urban Future
DCWP Releases Updated Research Brief: 238,900 Households in NYC Are Unbanked
